seoTitle: How to Format Tables in Google Docs Easily
metaDescription: Learn how to style tables in Google Docs with custom padding, borders, header colors, and even row distribution using Table Properties.
intro: A raw, unstyled table can look messy next to the rest of your document, but Google Docs gives you a dedicated Table Properties sidebar to polish it into something presentation-ready. This guide covers adjusting padding, applying header colors, tweaking borders, and evenly distributing rows and columns.

steps.heading: Open Table Properties
steps.body: Highlight your table, right-click the selection, and choose 'Table Properties' to open the formatting sidebar.

steps.heading: Set dimensions
steps.body: Expand the Table and Column sections to input precise numerical dimensions for your layout.

steps.heading: Adjust cell padding
steps.body: In the Cell dropdown, increase internal padding so text doesn't feel cramped against the borders.

steps.heading: Style the header row
steps.body: Highlight your top row and change its background color to create a strong visual hierarchy between headers and data.

steps.heading: Distribute rows and columns evenly
steps.body: Select the table, right-click, and choose 'Distribute rows' or 'Distribute columns' for uniform sizing across the grid.

faq.question: Can I add alternating row colors to my table?
faq.answer: Yes, you can manually apply background colors to alternating rows to create a zebra-striped effect.

faq.question: How do I change border thickness?
faq.answer: Use the toolbar's border icons after selecting your table, or adjust it more precisely inside Table Properties.

faq.question: Will formatting changes apply to the whole table or just selected cells?
faq.answer: Most changes apply to whatever cells or rows are currently selected, so you can style specific sections independently.

faq.question: What happens if my formatted table doesn't fit on one page?
faq.answer: Check your column widths and padding, and Google Docs will still let the table flow onto the next page while keeping formatting intact.
